text.title: Solo X Analytics Review Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for a weekly X / Twitter analytics review focused on indie audience growth.

rule.statement: The review covers exactly five metrics: (1) total impressions, (2) profile visits, (3) net followers (gained − lost), (4) replies from strangers (not in your existing network), (5) link clicks to owned URL (newsletter / product). Follower count alone is forbidden as the headline number.
rule.rationale: Impressions and follower counts move together for bot-attracting content. The "replies from strangers" + "link clicks" pair is what separates audience-growth from engagement theatre.

rule.statement: Total review time is capped at 20 minutes per week, on a fixed day (e.g., Friday afternoon). Within the slot: 8 min metric scan, 8 min identify the top + bottom post of the week, 4 min write the next-week experiment line. Running long means cutting depth, not extending.
rule.rationale: Audience analytics expand to fill any time slot. A 20-minute cap forces selecting signal over thorough analysis.

rule.statement: A post is an "outlier" only if its impressions are ≥3x the trailing-4-week median of the operator's own posts. Outliers are studied (what was different); non-outliers are NOT — comparing every individual post against another's algorithm-favoured post is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Solo operators waste hours studying posts that performed at baseline, mistaking randomness for signal. A 3x threshold focuses learning on posts where something real happened.

rule.statement: Each review produces exactly one named experiment for the following week, with a hypothesis, the variable changed, and the success metric. Examples: "post hook with concrete number → expect impressions ≥1.5x baseline", "thread length 5 vs 12 tweets → expect link-click rate same or higher with 5". Zero or multiple experiments are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Multiple simultaneous variable changes destroy the signal. One-at-a-time experimentation is the only way a solo operator builds a useful mental model of their audience over months.

rule.statement: "Net followers" in the dashboard is tagged: % qualified vs unqualified. Qualified = bio mentions ICP keyword, or has a clearly-aligned profile. Unqualified = generic / sales accounts / suspected bot. Reviews report both numbers; chasing total-follower numbers without qualified-follower discipline is rejected.
rule.rationale: Algorithm-bait posts attract unqualified followers at high rates. Without tagging, the operator optimises into a follower list that doesn't convert to product or newsletter readers.

example.code:
## X review — W20 (2026-05-16, 18 min)
Metrics (vs trailing 4w median): - Impressions: 82,400 (median 41,000) → 2.0x - Profile visits: 611 (median 390) → 1.6x - Net followers: +94 (qualified ~52% / unqualified ~48%) - Replies-from-strangers: 23 (median 11) → 2.1x - Link clicks: 184 (median 78) → 2.4x
Top post (outlier, 3.4x baseline): "I shipped pricing v2 in 4h after staring at it for 6 weeks. Here's the one-table reason I was stuck — sunk-cost fear of breaking the discount grandfather." (with screenshot) → variable: emotional-vulnerability + concrete artifact
Bottom post: technical tip with no hook, baseline performance.
NEXT-WEEK EXPERIMENT (W21): Hypothesis: emotional-vulnerability opener + concrete artifact → outlier rate ≥1 outlier per 6 posts (vs current ~1 per 12). Variable: open with personal feeling-fact in 3 of 6 posts. Success metric: ≥1 post ≥3x baseline by end of W21.
example.note: Five metrics, outlier rule applied (3.4x), one experiment with named hypothesis + variable + success metric, qualified-followers tagged.

antipattern.description: Operator reports "post X went viral with 50k impressions" without comparing to baseline, then concludes "longer threads work" and switches all posts to 12-tweet threads without controlling for the hook.
antipattern.reason: Violates r3 (no outlier comparison to baseline) and r4 (multiple variables changed in one experiment). Causes a 4-week confidence dip when the new format under-performs.
